text: I would like to present a technique for laparoscopic proctocolectomy and eyelid patching anastomosis. The patient is a twenty-one-year-old female who initially presented in nineteen ninety-six with bloody diarrhea. Colonoscopy revealed ulcerative proctitis, which was controlled with maintenance, six MP, and acical. In October ninety-nine, however, she had a flare. Prednisone was started at forty milligrams and tapered to twenty in addition to the six MP and Asacol. She however continued with frequent bloody bowel movements and colonoscopy revealed pancolitis. On two twenty three she underwent laparoscopic proctocolectomy, pouch and loop ileostomy. The operative time was three hundred and twenty four minutes in the EBL two hundred cc's. She was discharged three days later.

The surgeon begins by identifying the left ureter and opens the left lateral peritoneal reflection, retracting the left colon medially to stay in the correct plane. Proximal dissection allows for further traction and mobilization of the descending colon. At the left pelvic brim, attachments are divided to define the proximal presacral space. Moving towards the splenic flexure, lateral attachments are divided using medial retraction and careful dissection. The splenic flexure is mobilized off Girota's fascia and the left upper retroperitoneum, achieving full mobilization with partial omental mobilization.

The surgeon moves to the right side, revealing the pelvis with the uterus visible on the left. By elevating the small bowel mesentery, the ureter is visible beneath the peritoneum. The yellow fat contrasts with the white peritoneum, guiding the dissection plane as the peritoneum is scored. Lateral attachments along the peritoneal reflection are opened, fully mobilizing the cecum and exposing the lower right retroperitoneum. Tension is applied to the lateral attachments of the ascending colon, allowing further mobilization of the small bowel mesentery and exposing the right retroperitoneum, including the ureter, vena cava, and aorta. The patient is placed in reverse Trendelenburg position at the hepatic flexure. An anomalous appendix is observed with its tip near the liver. Hepatocolic attachments are divided using electrocautery or a harmonic scalpel, with minimal significant vessels present.

The surgeon identifies the duodenum and mobilizes the hepatic flexure off the retroperitoneum, ensuring protection of the duodenum. The transverse colon is sufficiently mobilized to be exteriorized through a peri-umbilical incision. The final attachments to the retroperitoneum are lysed, revealing the full right retroperitoneum. The procedure then shifts focus to rectal mobilization.

The surgeon continues the left lateral peritoneal dissection down into the left pararectal peritoneum, revealing the fine connective tissue of the proximal presacral plane. Using a laparoscopic approach, the nerves in this area are visible. The dissection then moves to the right side, where the presacral plane is connected, visible as a darker area on the peritoneum. The right pararectal peritoneum is scored, joining the presacral space dissection from the left side.

The surgeon uses a fan retractor to elevate the uterus, facilitating the initial dissection in the rectovaginal septum. The anterior attachments are lysed, increasing tension on the lateral attachments for easier dissection. The circumferential dissection around the rectum continues, exposing the pelvic floor behind the rectum. Smoke clearance is challenging due to the mobilized colon blocking the pelvic inlet.

The surgeon mobilizes anterior attachments to facilitate stapler deployment, enhancing tension on lateral attachments for clearer visualization of the pelvic floor. The posterior wall of the vagina is carefully dissected off the rectum, with the vagina visible anteriorly. The pelvic floor muscles are seen on either side, and posterior attachments are lysed to position the stapler at the pelvic floor level. An articulated stapler is placed across the distal rectum, and tension is applied to tent the rectum, allowing transection at the levators' level.

The surgeon extends the supraumbilical port site incision around the umbilicus to exteriorize the colon, from the terminal ileum to the transected rectum. The mesentery is divided extracorporeally, and the small bowel is exteriorized to check for Crohn's disease. The incision allows visualization of the duodenum, and the small bowel mesentery attachments are lysed. A J-pouch is created using a linear stapler, and the EEA stapler spike is inserted through the anus. The pouch is brought down to the stapler after reestablishing pneumoperitoneum, with the posterior vaginal wall elevated for stapling completion.
